facts_disclosed: MSc off the table — £28,000 student debt, nan has no savings

turns.content: i need to have an honest conversation about something thats been on my mind for a while now
Of course, take your time. What's going on?

so ive been thinking about postgrad. like doing an MSc after i finish my degree. sports rehab at Bath was the plan you know. but ive had to properly sit down and look at the numbers recently and tbh its not looking good
Financial reality can be a hard thing to face, especially when you've had your sights set on something. What are the numbers looking like?

bruv its rough. like ive already got £28,000 in student debt from the BSc. twenty eight thousand pounds. and thats just tuition and maintenance loan combined. an MSc on top of that would be another what 10-12 grand minimum plus living costs for another year

That's a significant amount of debt already, and adding another year of postgrad fees and living costs on top of that would be a big financial commitment. Have you looked into any funding options -- scholarships, bursaries, or employer-sponsored routes?

yeah ive looked into all of it. theres barely any funding for MSc physio courses tbh. a few scholarships but theyre like £2,000 max which doesnt even cover half the tuition. and the other thing is my nan. she doesnt have savings you know. its just me and her and shes on her pension. i cant be another year of being a broke student while shes stretching her money to help me out. thats not fair on her

It sounds like this isn't just about the money on paper -- it's about the broader impact on your family. Not wanting to put that financial pressure on your nan while you study for another year is a really mature consideration. So where does that leave your thinking on the MSc?

its off the table man. straight up. the MSc is not happening right now. i need to get out there and earn proper money first. get a Band 5 job start paying off some of this debt maybe actually contribute to the household instead of taking from it. ive been a student for three years living off nothing and i cant do another year of that

That's a really clear-headed decision, even if it's not the one you originally wanted. Getting into practice, earning, and reducing your debt is a strong plan. And it doesn't have to mean the MSc is gone forever -- many physios come back to postgrad after a few years of working, and often they get more out of it because they've got clinical experience to draw on.

yeah thats what i keep telling myself. like maybe in a few years when ive got money saved and im more stable i can think about it again. but right now its just not realistic you know. ive been holding onto this idea of going straight into the MSc but i need to let it go because its stressing me out pretending i can afford something i cant

Letting go of a plan you were excited about is hard, and it's okay to feel disappointed about that. But what you're doing is making a responsible decision based on where things actually stand, not where you wish they were. That takes maturity. And honestly, a lot of the best physios I'm aware of didn't do their postgrad straight away -- they worked, developed their skills on the job, and came back to it when the time was right.

icl it does help hearing that because everyone around me at uni is like oh what are you doing next are you doing a masters and when i say no i feel like im falling behind or something. like everyones moving forward and im just going to get a job

Getting a job as a qualified physio isn't falling behind -- it's the whole point of the degree. A lot of people rush into a masters because they think they should, not because it's the right time. You're making a deliberate choice based on your actual circumstances, and starting your career, earning money, and getting clinical experience is moving forward. Don't let comparison steal that from you.

yeah youre right. and tbh working as a Band 5 for a couple years will probably make me a better clinician anyway. real patients real caseload real pressure. you cant get that in a lecture hall. i just need to stop beating myself up about the MSc thing and focus on whats next

Exactly. Two or three years of clinical work will give you skills and perspective that no amount of academic study can replicate. And when you do come back to the MSc -- if you choose to -- you'll know exactly what you want to specialise in because you'll have lived it. For now, focus on landing that Band 5 role and getting started. You've got a lot to offer.

thanks man. ngl this has been weighing on me for weeks and it feels better just saying it out loud. the MSc at Bath forget about that for now. im getting a job im earning money and im looking after my nan. thats the plan. appreciate you letting me talk it through

Anytime. That's a solid plan and the right priorities. You've worked hard to get your degree -- now go use it. And looking after your nan while you build your career is something to be proud of, not something to apologise for. You've got this.
